importance_note: pH scale is a guaranteed question in Chemistry. Application examples from daily life are required for full marks.

question: What is the common name and chemical formula of (i) Washing soda, (ii) Baking soda, (iii) Bleaching powder, (iv) Plaster of Paris? Write one use for each.
subject: science
answer: (i) Washing soda – Na₂CO₃·10H₂O – used for cleaning, softening hard water. (ii) Baking soda – NaHCO₃ – used in baking powder, antacids. (iii) Bleaching powder – CaOCl₂ – used for disinfecting water, bleaching cotton. (iv) Plaster of Paris – CaSO₄·½H₂O – used for plastering fractured bones, making casts.
importance_note: Compounds of sodium, calcium and their uses appear in every board paper. Usually 1 mark each for formula + use.

question: Differentiate between soaps and detergents. Why do soaps not work in hard water? Explain micelle formation in cleaning action of soap.
subject: science
answer: Soaps are sodium/potassium salts of long-chain fatty acids (biodegradable); detergents are sodium salts of sulphonic acids (may not be biodegradable). Soaps in hard water: Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ ions react with soap to form insoluble scum (calcium/magnesium stearate), making soap ineffective. Detergents work in hard water. Micelle formation: soap molecules have a hydrophilic (water-loving) head and hydrophobic (water-repelling) tail. In water, they arrange with tails pointing inward enclosing the grease/oil droplet, and heads pointing outward toward water – forming a cluster called a micelle – which can be washed away with water.
importance_note: Soaps vs detergents and micelle formation is one of the highest-scoring 5-mark questions in Carbon Compounds. Appears almost every year.

question: What is esterification? Write the reaction between ethanol and ethanoic acid with conditions. How can you distinguish between ethanol and ethanoic acid using a chemical test?
subject: science
answer: Esterification: Reaction of a carboxylic acid with an alcohol in presence of concentrated H₂SO₄ to form an ester and water. CH₃COOH + C₂H₅OH ⇌ CH₃COOC₂H₅ + H₂O (conc. H₂SO₄, heat). Distinguishing test: Add Na₂CO₃ solution – ethanoic acid produces brisk effervescence (CO₂ gas); ethanol shows no reaction. OR: Add blue litmus – ethanoic acid turns it red; ethanol does not.
importance_note: Esterification reaction and distinguishing tests for ethanol vs ethanoic acid are standard 3-mark board questions.

question: What is a homologous series? Give two characteristics. Write the IUPAC names of: CH₃CHO, CH₃COCH₃, CH₃CH₂OH, CH₃COOH.
subject: science
answer: Homologous series: A series of organic compounds with the same functional group, same general formula, and differing by –CH₂– (14 mass units) each time. Characteristics: (1) Each member differs by –CH₂– from next. (2) Physical properties vary gradually; chemical properties are similar. IUPAC names: CH₃CHO = Ethanal; CH₃COCH₃ = Propanone; CH₃CH₂OH = Ethanol; CH₃COOH = Ethanoic acid.
importance_note: Homologous series definition and IUPAC nomenclature questions appear every year. Nomenclature is very high frequency in Carbon Compounds.

question: Why is carbon able to form an extremely large number of compounds? Explain with reference to catenation and tetravalency.
subject: science
topic: properties of carbon – catenation and tetravalency
answer: Carbon forms a huge number of compounds due to: (1) Catenation – carbon atoms can bond with each other to form long chains, branched chains, and rings. This is possible because C–C bonds are very strong and stable. (2) Tetravalency – carbon has a valency of 4, so it can form four covalent bonds simultaneously with other carbon atoms or atoms of other elements (H, O, N, S, Cl). These two properties together allow the formation of millions of organic compounds.
importance_note: This conceptual question is asked almost every year as a 2–3 mark question. It is the foundational concept of the entire Carbon chapter.

question: Draw a neat labelled diagram of the human heart. Explain the process of double circulation in humans.
subject: science
answer: Labelled diagram should show: right and left atria, right and left ventricles, aorta, pulmonary artery, pulmonary vein, vena cava, valves (tricuspid, bicuspid, semi-lunar). Double circulation: Blood passes through the heart twice per complete circuit. (1) Pulmonary circulation – deoxygenated blood from right ventricle goes to lungs via pulmonary artery; oxygenated blood returns to left atrium via pulmonary vein. (2) Systemic circulation – oxygenated blood from left ventricle goes to body via aorta; deoxygenated blood returns to right atrium via vena cava. Advantage: complete separation of oxygenated and deoxygenated blood ensuring efficient oxygen delivery.
importance_note: The human heart diagram with double circulation is the single most frequently asked 5-mark Biology question in CBSE Class 10 boards.

question: Draw a neat labelled diagram of the human nephron. Explain the process of urine formation.
subject: science
answer: Nephron diagram labels: Bowman's capsule, glomerulus, proximal convoluted tubule (PCT), loop of Henle, distal convoluted tubule (DCT), collecting duct, afferent and efferent arterioles. Urine formation – 3 steps: (1) Filtration – blood under pressure is filtered in glomerulus; filtrate enters Bowman's capsule containing water, salts, glucose, urea. (2) Reabsorption – useful substances (glucose, amino acids, water, salts) are reabsorbed from PCT and loop of Henle back into blood. (3) Secretion – remaining waste (creatinine, extra H⁺) is secreted into filtrate in DCT. Final urine passes through collecting duct to ureter.
importance_note: Nephron diagram and urine formation is the second most frequently asked 5-mark Biology question. Almost guaranteed in board exams.

question: Draw a labelled diagram of the human brain. Name and state the functions of its three main parts.
subject: science
answer: Brain diagram labels: cerebrum, cerebellum, medulla oblongata, pons, mid-brain. Functions: (1) Cerebrum (forebrain) – voluntary actions, thinking, memory, intelligence, consciousness, vision, speech. (2) Cerebellum (hindbrain) – coordinates muscular movements, maintains posture and balance. (3) Medulla oblongata (hindbrain) – controls involuntary actions like heartbeat, breathing, blood pressure, vomiting.
importance_note: Human brain diagram appears frequently as a 5-mark question. Also asked as 3-mark with function-only format.

question: Differentiate between aerobic and anaerobic respiration. Where does anaerobic respiration occur in human muscles during vigorous activity and what is its product?
subject: science
answer: Aerobic respiration: occurs with oxygen, in cytoplasm and mitochondria, produces CO₂ + H₂O + 38 ATP (high energy). Anaerobic respiration: occurs without oxygen, only in cytoplasm, produces lactic acid (in muscles) or ethanol + CO₂ (in yeast), releases only 2 ATP. In human muscles during vigorous exercise, insufficient oxygen leads to anaerobic respiration, producing lactic acid – causing muscle cramps.
importance_note: Aerobic vs anaerobic respiration is asked in 2–3 mark format nearly every year. Lactic acid cramp connection is a common 1-mark quick question.

question: How does photosynthesis occur? Write the overall chemical equation. Name the two stages of photosynthesis and state what happens in each.
subject: science
answer: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ (in presence of sunlight and chlorophyll). Two stages: (1) Light reaction (in grana of chloroplast) – chlorophyll absorbs light energy; water splits (photolysis) releasing O₂; ATP and NADPH are produced. (2) Dark reaction / Calvin cycle (in stroma) – CO₂ is fixed using ATP and NADPH to produce glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆). Does not directly require light.
importance_note: Photosynthesis equation and stages are asked every year. The equation alone is 1 mark; detailed explanation is 3–5 marks.

question: Draw a labelled diagram of the neuron (nerve cell). Explain the transmission of a nerve impulse.
subject: science
answer: Neuron diagram labels: cell body (cyton), nucleus, dendrites, axon, myelin sheath, nodes of Ranvier, axon terminals (synaptic knob). Nerve impulse transmission: Dendrites receive stimulus → electrical impulse travels along axon → at synapse (junction), neurotransmitters are released → they cross the synaptic cleft → bind to receptors on next neuron's dendrites → impulse continues. Reflex arc involves: receptor → sensory neuron → spinal cord → motor neuron → effector (muscle/gland).
importance_note: Neuron diagram is a high-frequency 5-mark question. Often combined with reflex arc in board exams.

question: What is Mendel's Law of Segregation? Explain a monohybrid cross between a tall (TT) pea plant and a dwarf (tt) pea plant. What are the phenotypic and genotypic ratios in F2 generation?
subject: science
answer: Law of Segregation: Alleles for a trait separate during gamete formation so each gamete carries only one allele for each trait. Monohybrid cross: P: TT (tall) × tt (dwarf). F1: All Tt (tall – dominant). F1 × F1: Tt × Tt. F2 Punnett square: TT : Tt : tt = 1:2:1. Phenotypic ratio = 3 tall : 1 dwarf. Genotypic ratio = 1TT : 2Tt : 1tt. Tall is dominant over dwarf.
importance_note: Monohybrid cross with Punnett square is the most tested Heredity question – 5 marks, appears in nearly every board paper.

question: How is sex determined in human beings? Explain with the help of a diagram showing sex chromosomes in parents and offspring.
subject: science
answer: Humans have 46 chromosomes (23 pairs). Females: 44 autosomes + XX (sex chromosomes). Males: 44 autosomes + XY. During reproduction, all eggs carry X chromosome. Sperm can carry either X or Y. If X-sperm fertilises egg → XX = daughter. If Y-sperm fertilises egg → XY = son. Therefore, sex of child is determined by the father's sperm, not the mother. Probability of boy/girl is always 50:50.
importance_note: Sex determination in humans is asked every year as 3-mark question. Blaming mother for child's sex is a social misconception this question addresses.

question: What are analogous and homologous organs? Give one example of each. What do they indicate about evolution?
subject: science
answer: Homologous organs: Same structural origin and basic anatomy but perform different functions. Example: human arm, whale flipper, bat wing, frog forelimb – all have same bone arrangement (humerus, radius, ulna). Indicate divergent evolution from a common ancestor. Analogous organs: Different structural origin but perform similar functions. Example: wings of a bird and wings of an insect. Indicate convergent evolution – different organisms evolving similar features to adapt to similar environments.
importance_note: Homologous vs analogous organs is a standard 3-mark question in Heredity and Evolution, tested almost every year.

question: Explain the asexual reproduction method of (i) binary fission in Amoeba, (ii) budding in Hydra, (iii) spore formation in Rhizopus. Draw diagrams for any two.
subject: science
answer: (i) Binary fission (Amoeba): Cell elongates → nucleus divides (mitosis) → cytoplasm divides → two daughter cells. Each daughter is genetically identical. (ii) Budding (Hydra): A small outgrowth (bud) appears on parent body → develops into a miniature Hydra → detaches to form new organism. (iii) Spore formation (Rhizopus): Sporangium (spore case) forms at tips of hyphae → spores develop inside → sporangium bursts under suitable conditions → spores dispersed by air/water → germinate into new Rhizopus.
importance_note: Asexual reproduction methods with diagrams are asked in 3–5 mark format. Binary fission and budding are the most tested.

question: Explain the process of fertilisation in flowering plants. What happens to the different parts of the flower after fertilisation?
subject: science
answer: Pollination transfers pollen grain to stigma. Pollen germinates, forming pollen tube through style into ovary → reaches ovule. Male gamete travels through pollen tube → fuses with egg cell in ovule (fertilisation/syngamy) → forms zygote. Post-fertilisation changes: Zygote → embryo; ovule → seed; ovary wall → fruit (pericarp); petals, stamens, sepals wither and fall off; calyx may persist in some plants.
importance_note: Fertilisation in flowering plants and post-fertilisation changes are tested every year in 3-mark format.

question: What is the mirror formula? An object is placed 15 cm in front of a concave mirror of focal length 10 cm. Find the position and nature of the image.
subject: science
answer: Mirror formula: 1/v + 1/u = 1/f. Sign convention: distances measured from pole; in front of mirror is negative. Given: u = –15 cm, f = –10 cm (concave mirror). 1/v = 1/f – 1/u = 1/(–10) – 1/(–15) = –1/10 + 1/15 = (–3+2)/30 = –1/30. So v = –30 cm. Image is formed 30 cm in front of the mirror (real, inverted, magnified). Magnification m = –v/u = –(–30)/(–15) = –2 (inverted, twice the size).
importance_note: Mirror formula numericals are asked in almost every board paper alongside lens formula questions.

question: What is refraction of light? State Snell's Law. The refractive index of glass with respect to air is 1.5. What is the refractive index of air with respect to glass?
subject: science
answer: Refraction: Bending of light as it passes from one transparent medium to another due to change in speed. Snell's Law: sin i / sin r = n₂/n₁ = constant (where i = angle of incidence, r = angle of refraction). Refractive index of glass w.r.t. air = n_g = 1.5. Refractive index of air w.r.t. glass = 1/n_g = 1/1.5 = 2/3 ≈ 0.67.
importance_note: Refraction, Snell's Law, and refractive index reciprocal relationship are tested in every alternate year as 2–3 mark questions.

question: What is the power of a lens? A convex lens has focal length 25 cm and a concave lens has focal length 50 cm. What is the power of the combination when placed in contact?
subject: science
answer: Power of a lens (P) = 1/f (in metres). Unit: dioptre (D). Convex lens: f = +25 cm = +0.25 m → P₁ = +4 D. Concave lens: f = –50 cm = –0.5 m → P₂ = –2 D. Combined power = P₁ + P₂ = 4 + (–2) = +2 D. Positive value means the combination acts as a convex (converging) lens.
importance_note: Power of lens and combination of lenses is a standard numerical asked in 2–3 mark format every year.

question: State the differences between the action of acids and bases on indicators: (i) litmus, (ii) phenolphthalein, (iii) methyl orange. Which of these is present in turmeric?
subject: science
answer: Indicator | In Acid | In Base. (i) Litmus: turns red | turns blue. (ii) Phenolphthalein: colourless | pink/magenta. (iii) Methyl orange: turns red/pink | turns yellow. Turmeric: Turmeric stains are not washed out by soap (a base); the stain turns reddish-brown in base (turmeric contains curcumin, a natural indicator). None of the three listed indicators is in turmeric; turmeric itself is a natural indicator.
importance_note: Indicators and their colour changes are very commonly tested in 1-mark MCQ and 3-mark short answer questions.

question: What is corrosion? Give two methods to prevent corrosion of iron. Write the chemical formula of rust.
subject: science
answer: Corrosion: Slow deterioration of metals due to reaction with moisture, oxygen or other chemicals in the environment. Example: Iron rusts, silver tarnishes, copper turns green. Chemical formula of rust: Fe₂O₃·xH₂O (hydrated iron(III) oxide). Prevention: (1) Painting or greasing the iron surface to prevent contact with oxygen/moisture. (2) Galvanisation – coating iron with zinc (zinc is more reactive, acts as sacrificial anode). Others: alloying, electroplating, using anti-rust solutions.
importance_note: Corrosion and rancidity are commonly asked 2–3 mark questions. Rust formula and prevention methods appear in multiple years.

question: Explain with an activity how you can show that carbon dioxide is produced during respiration.
subject: science
answer: Activity: Take a flask with germinating seeds. Pass exhaled air (or air from the flask) through lime water [Ca(OH)₂ solution] using a delivery tube. Observation: Lime water turns milky. Inference: CO₂ is produced during respiration, as CO₂ reacts with lime water – Ca(OH)₂ + CO₂ → CaCO₃ (white precipitate) + H₂O. Conclusion: Respiration produces carbon dioxide.
importance_note: Activity-based questions with observations and inferences are a growing part of CBSE boards (3-mark format). Respiration CO₂ test is classic.

question: What are the advantages and disadvantages of sexual reproduction over asexual reproduction?
subject: science
answer: Advantages of sexual reproduction over asexual: (1) Produces genetic variation in offspring (combining DNA from two parents) → helps adaptation and evolution. (2) Variations help species survive changing environments. (3) Harmful mutations can be masked by dominant alleles. Disadvantages: (1) Requires finding a mate – needs more energy and time. (2) Produces fewer offspring compared to asexual reproduction. (3) More complex process. Asexual reproduction advantage: faster, requires only one parent, produces large numbers quickly.
importance_note: Comparison between sexual and asexual reproduction is a standard 3-mark question in the Reproduction chapter.

question: What is electromagnetic induction? State Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction. Give two applications.
subject: science
answer: Electromagnetic induction: The phenomenon of generating an electric current in a conductor by changing the magnetic flux through it. Faraday's Law: (1) An EMF is induced in a conductor when there is a change in magnetic flux linked with it. (2) The magnitude of the induced EMF is directly proportional to the rate of change of magnetic flux. Applications: (1) AC generator / dynamo – converts mechanical energy to electrical energy. (2) Electric transformer – steps up or steps down AC voltage.
importance_note: Electromagnetic induction and Faraday's law is tested in 3-mark format. Applications are required for full marks.

question: A student wants to project the image of a candle flame on a screen 60 cm away using a convex lens of focal length 15 cm. At what distance should she place the candle from the lens? What is the nature and magnification of the image?
subject: science
answer: Given: v = +60 cm, f = +15 cm. Using lens formula: 1/u = 1/v – 1/f = 1/60 – 1/15 = (1–4)/60 = –3/60 = –1/20. So u = –20 cm. Place candle 20 cm in front of the lens. Magnification m = v/u = 60/(–20) = –3. Nature: real, inverted, magnified (3× the object size).
importance_note: Practical scenario-based lens formula numericals are increasingly common in CBSE boards as competency-based questions.

question: Draw a labelled diagram of the human heart and explain the circulation of blood through it. Why is human circulation called 'double circulation'?
subject: science
answer:
The human heart is a four-chambered muscular organ divided into right atrium, right ventricle, left atrium, and left ventricle. It is divided by a septum that prevents mixing of oxygenated and deoxygenated blood.

Diagram labels: Right Atrium, Right Ventricle, Left Atrium, Left Ventricle, Superior Vena Cava, Inferior Vena Cava, Pulmonary Artery, Pulmonary Vein, Aorta, Bicuspid valve (mitral), Tricuspid valve, Semi-lunar valves.

Blood flow explanation:
1. PULMONARY CIRCUIT: Deoxygenated blood from the body enters the right atrium via superior and inferior vena cava → passes to right ventricle → pumped to lungs via pulmonary artery → oxygenated in lungs.
2. SYSTEMIC CIRCUIT: Oxygenated blood returns from lungs via pulmonary veins into left atrium → passes to left ventricle → pumped to entire body via aorta.

Double circulation: Blood passes through the heart twice in one complete cycle — once through the pulmonary circuit (heart → lungs → heart) and once through the systemic circuit (heart → body → heart). This ensures complete separation of oxygenated and deoxygenated blood, providing efficient oxygen supply to all body tissues.

question: Draw a labelled diagram of the human digestive system and describe the role of each organ in the digestion of food.
subject: science
answer:
Diagram labels: Mouth (buccal cavity), Salivary glands (parotid, sublingual, submandibular), Oesophagus, Stomach, Liver, Gall bladder, Pancreas, Small intestine (duodenum, jejunum, ileum), Large intestine (colon), Rectum, Anus.

Role of each organ:
1. Mouth: Teeth mechanically break food. Salivary glands secrete saliva containing salivary amylase (ptyalin) which begins digestion of starch into maltose.
2. Oesophagus: Transports food to the stomach by peristaltic movements. No digestion occurs here.
3. Stomach: Secretes gastric juice containing HCl (kills bacteria, activates pepsinogen), pepsin (digests proteins into peptides), and mucus (protects stomach lining). Food is churned into chyme.
4. Liver: Produces bile juice (stored in gall bladder) which emulsifies fats into smaller droplets (no enzyme, physical action) and neutralises the acidic chyme.
5. Pancreas: Secretes pancreatic juice containing trypsin (digests proteins), lipase (digests fats into fatty acids + glycerol), and amylase (digests starch).
6. Small intestine: Intestinal juice (succus entericus) completes digestion of all food types. Finger-like villi increase surface area for absorption of digested food into blood capillaries (sugars, amino acids) and lacteals (fatty acids, glycerol).
7. Large intestine: Absorbs water and minerals from undigested material; forms faeces.
8. Rectum/Anus: Temporarily stores and then expels faeces.

question: Draw a labelled diagram of a nephron and explain the process of urine formation.
subject: science
answer:
Diagram labels: Bowman's capsule, Glomerulus, Afferent arteriole, Efferent arteriole, Proximal convoluted tubule (PCT), Loop of Henle (descending and ascending limb), Distal convoluted tubule (DCT), Collecting duct, Peritubular capillaries.

Urine formation occurs in three stages:
1. ULTRAFILTRATION: Blood enters glomerulus through afferent arteriole under high pressure. Small molecules — water, glucose, urea, uric acid, salts, amino acids — are filtered into Bowman's capsule forming the glomerular filtrate. Large molecules like proteins and RBCs remain in blood.
2. SELECTIVE REABSORPTION: As filtrate passes through PCT, loop of Henle, and DCT, useful substances are reabsorbed back into surrounding peritubular capillaries: all glucose and amino acids, most water, necessary salts. This occurs by active transport and osmosis.
3. SECRETION: Additional waste products like creatinine, excess salts, and H⁺ ions are secreted from blood into the tubule.

The concentrated urine flows from collecting duct → renal pelvis → ureter → urinary bladder → urethra → expelled from body.

question: Draw a labelled diagram of a neuron. Explain how nerve impulse is transmitted from one neuron to another.
subject: science
answer:
Diagram labels: Cell body (soma/cyton), Nucleus, Dendrites, Axon, Myelin sheath, Node of Ranvier, Axon terminals (synaptic knobs), Synapse.

Neuron structure: The cell body contains the nucleus and cytoplasm. Dendrites are short branched extensions that receive nerve impulses. The axon is a long single fibre that carries impulse away from cell body. Myelin sheath insulates the axon and speeds up impulse conduction. Nodes of Ranvier are gaps in the myelin sheath.

Transmission of nerve impulse:
1. Within a neuron: When a stimulus is received at dendrites, an electrochemical change (action potential) is generated. This electrical impulse travels along the axon from dendrite → cell body → axon terminals.
2. At the synapse (junction between two neurons): When the impulse reaches the axon terminals (synaptic knob), it triggers release of chemical neurotransmitters (e.g., acetylcholine) into the synaptic cleft. These chemicals diffuse across the cleft and bind to receptors on the dendrites of the next neuron, generating a new electrical impulse. This is called synaptic transmission.

The impulse thus moves: Stimulus → Receptor → Sensory neuron → Relay neuron (spinal cord/brain) → Motor neuron → Effector (muscle/gland).

question: In Mendel's monohybrid cross experiment with pea plants, a pure tall plant (TT) was crossed with a pure dwarf plant (tt). (a) Draw a Punnett square showing the F1 and F2 generations. (b) State the ratio of tall to dwarf plants in F2. (c) What is the significance of this experiment?
subject: science
answer:
F1 Cross — TT × tt:
Punnett square:
     T    T
  t [Tt] [Tt]
  t [Tt] [Tt]
All F1 plants are Tt (tall) — only dominant trait is expressed.

F2 Cross — Tt × Tt (self-pollination of F1):
Punnett square:
     T    t
  T [TT] [Tt]
  t [Tt] [tt]
Genotypes: 1TT : 2Tt : 1tt
Phenotypes: 3 Tall : 1 Dwarf

(b) The ratio of tall to dwarf plants in F2 is 3:1 (phenotypic ratio). The genotypic ratio is 1:2:1.

(c) Significance:
1. Law of Dominance: In F1, only the dominant character (tallness) appears while recessive (dwarfness) remains hidden.
2. Law of Segregation: In F2, the recessive character reappears, proving that alleles separate during gamete formation and do not blend permanently.
3. Concept of hereditary units (genes): Traits are controlled by factors (genes) that are inherited from both parents, one from each.
4. This experiment formed the foundation of modern genetics.

question: Draw ray diagrams to show the formation of image when an object is placed (i) between centre of curvature and focus of a concave mirror, and (ii) beyond the centre of curvature of a concave mirror. State the position, nature, and size of the image in each case.
subject: science
answer:
Case (i) — Object between C and F:
Ray diagram: Draw concave mirror. Object AB between C and F.
- Ray 1: Parallel to principal axis → reflects through focus F.
- Ray 2: Through centre of curvature C → reflects back along same path.
- Intersection: Beyond C, on the same side as the object.
Image: Beyond C, Real, Inverted, Magnified (larger than object).

Case (ii) — Object beyond C:
Ray diagram: Object AB beyond C.
- Ray 1: Parallel to principal axis → reflects through focus F.
- Ray 2: Through centre of curvature C → reflects back along same path.
- Intersection: Between C and F, on same side as object.
Image: Between C and F, Real, Inverted, Diminished (smaller than object).

Key rules used in ray diagrams:
1. A ray parallel to the principal axis reflects through the principal focus.
2. A ray passing through the centre of curvature reflects back along the same path.
3. A ray through the principal focus reflects parallel to the principal axis.

question: Draw ray diagrams to show image formation by a convex lens when the object is placed (i) at 2F and (ii) between F and optical centre. State the nature, position, and size of the image in each case.
subject: science
answer:
Case (i) — Object at 2F:
Ray diagram: Draw convex lens with principal axis. Object AB placed at 2F₁ (left side).
- Ray 1: Parallel to principal axis → refracts through focus F₂.
- Ray 2: Through optical centre O → passes straight (no bending).
- Intersection: At 2F₂ on the other side.
Image: At 2F₂ (opposite side), Real, Inverted, Same size as object.

Case (ii) — Object between F and optical centre:
Ray diagram: Object AB placed between F₁ and optical centre O.
- Ray 1: Parallel to principal axis → refracts through F₂.
- Ray 2: Through optical centre O → passes straight.
- Rays diverge after refraction; extend backwards (dotted lines) to find virtual intersection.
Image: On the same side as object, behind object, Virtual, Erect, Magnified.

Application: This is the principle of a simple magnifying glass — placing the object between F and O gives an erect, virtual, magnified image.

question: A concave mirror has a focal length of 15 cm. An object of height 4 cm is placed 30 cm in front of the mirror. Find (i) the position of the image, (ii) the size of the image. Draw the ray diagram.
subject: science
topic: concave mirror numerical with ray diagram
answer:
Given: f = −15 cm (concave mirror), u = −30 cm (object in front), h = 4 cm.

Using mirror formula: 1/v + 1/u = 1/f
1/v + 1/(−30) = 1/(−15)
1/v = −1/15 + 1/30 = −2/30 + 1/30 = −1/30
v = −30 cm

(i) Image is formed 30 cm in front of the mirror (negative sign means in front → real image).

Magnification: m = −v/u = −(−30)/(−30) = −1

(ii) Image height = m × object height = −1 × 4 = −4 cm
The image is 4 cm tall. Negative sign means inverted.

Nature: Real, inverted, same size as object, formed at the centre of curvature C.

Ray diagram: Object AB at C (30 cm). Ray through C reflects back on itself. Ray parallel to axis reflects through F. Both rays meet at C on the same side. Image A'B' is real, inverted, same size at C.

question: State and explain Ohm's Law. Draw a labelled circuit diagram to verify Ohm's Law in the laboratory. Sketch the V-I graph and explain how resistance is determined from it.
subject: science
answer:
Ohm's Law: At constant temperature, the potential difference (V) across a metallic conductor is directly proportional to the current (I) flowing through it.
Mathematically: V ∝ I → V = IR, where R is the resistance of the conductor.

Circuit diagram components: Battery (B), Rheostat (Rh), Plug key (K), Ammeter (A) — in series; Voltmeter (V) — in parallel across the conductor/resistor under test.

Procedure to verify:
1. Set up the circuit with a resistor.
2. Use the rheostat to vary current through the circuit.
3. Record corresponding values of voltmeter (V) and ammeter (I) readings.
4. Calculate V/I for each reading — it remains constant = R.

V-I Graph:
- Plot V on Y-axis, I on X-axis.
- A straight line passing through the origin is obtained.
- The slope of the line = V/I = R (resistance).

Conclusion: Since V/I = constant and the graph is a straight line through origin, Ohm's Law is verified. Resistance R = slope of V-I graph = V/I (in ohms, Ω).

question: Derive the expression for the equivalent resistance when three resistors R₁, R₂, and R₃ are connected in series. Draw the circuit diagram. If R₁ = 3Ω, R₂ = 4Ω, R₃ = 5Ω are connected in series across a 12V battery, find (i) equivalent resistance (ii) current through circuit (iii) potential difference across each resistor.
subject: science
answer:
Circuit diagram: Battery (V) connected to R₁, R₂, R₃ in series with switch K and ammeter A.

Derivation:
In series connection, same current I flows through all resistors.
Potential difference across each: V₁ = IR₁, V₂ = IR₂, V₃ = IR₃.
Total potential difference: V = V₁ + V₂ + V₃ = IR₁ + IR₂ + IR₃ = I(R₁ + R₂ + R₃).
If Rₛ is equivalent resistance: V = IRₛ
Therefore: Rₛ = R₁ + R₂ + R₃

Numerical:
Given: R₁ = 3Ω, R₂ = 4Ω, R₃ = 5Ω, V = 12V.

(i) Equivalent resistance: Rₛ = 3 + 4 + 5 = 12Ω

(ii) Current: I = V/Rₛ = 12/12 = 1A

(iii) Potential difference across each:
V₁ = IR₁ = 1 × 3 = 3V
V₂ = IR₂ = 1 × 4 = 4V
V₃ = IR₃ = 1 × 5 = 5V
Check: V₁ + V₂ + V₃ = 3 + 4 + 5 = 12V ✓

question: Derive the expression for equivalent resistance when three resistors R₁, R₂, and R₃ are connected in parallel. An electric iron consumes energy at a rate of 840W when heating and 360W when keeping warm. Find the current flowing through its resistive element at 220V. At what rate is heat developed in each case?
subject: science
topic: parallel resistance derivation and power numerical
answer:
Circuit diagram: Battery connected to R₁, R₂, R₃ in parallel — each resistor directly connected between the two common nodes.

Derivation:
In parallel, same potential difference V appears across each resistor.
Current through each: I₁ = V/R₁, I₂ = V/R₂, I₃ = V/R₃.
Total current: I = I₁ + I₂ + I₃ = V(1/R₁ + 1/R₂ + 1/R₃).
If Rₚ is equivalent resistance: I = V/Rₚ
Therefore: 1/Rₚ = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂ + 1/R₃

Numerical:
Given: V = 220V, P₁ (heating) = 840W, P₂ (warm) = 360W.
Using P = VI:

Heating mode: I₁ = P₁/V = 840/220 = 3.82 A
Heat developed = 840 W (rate = 840 J/s)

Warming mode: I₂ = P₂/V = 360/220 = 1.636 A
Heat developed = 360 W (rate = 360 J/s)

question: Draw a labelled diagram of the human eye. Explain the defect of vision called myopia. Draw ray diagrams to show (i) the defect and (ii) how it is corrected using a suitable lens.
subject: science
chapter: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
answer:
Eye diagram labels: Cornea, Iris, Pupil, Crystalline lens, Ciliary muscles, Aqueous humour, Vitreous humour, Retina (with fovea), Optic nerve, Blind spot.

Myopia (Short-sightedness):
Definition: A person with myopia can see nearby objects clearly but cannot see distant objects distinctly.
Cause: (i) Increase in length of the eyeball, or (ii) increase in curvature (decrease in focal length) of the eye lens. Image of distant object forms in front of the retina.

Ray diagram — Defect: Parallel rays from a distant object converge before reaching the retina (in front of retina).

Correction: A concave (diverging) lens of appropriate power is used. The concave lens diverges the incoming rays before they enter the eye, so they now appear to come from the near point of the defective eye and converge on the retina.

Ray diagram — Correction: Parallel rays → diverged by concave lens → converge exactly on retina.

Correction lens: Concave lens. Power = negative. The focal length of the corrective lens = far point of the myopic eye.

question: What is refraction of light? State the laws of refraction. A ray of light passes from air into a glass slab making an angle of 30° with the normal. If the refractive index of glass is 1.5, find the angle of refraction. Draw the ray diagram.
subject: science
answer:
Refraction: The bending of light as it travels from one transparent medium to another (due to change in speed) is called refraction.

Laws of Refraction:
1. The incident ray, refracted ray, and normal at the point of incidence all lie in the same plane.
2. Snell's Law: The ratio of sine of angle of incidence (i) to sine of angle of refraction (r) is constant for a given pair of media.
Mathematically: sin i / sin r = n₂₁ (refractive index of medium 2 w.r.t. medium 1).

Numerical:
Given: Angle of incidence i = 30°, refractive index n (glass w.r.t. air) = 1.5.
Using Snell's Law: n = sin i / sin r
1.5 = sin 30° / sin r
1.5 = 0.5 / sin r
sin r = 0.5 / 1.5 = 0.333
r = sin⁻¹(0.333) ≈ 19.47° ≈ 19.5°

Ray diagram: Draw air-glass boundary (flat surface). Normal perpendicular to boundary. Incident ray at 30° to normal in air, bending towards the normal in glass at r = 19.5° (glass is optically denser, so ray bends toward normal).

question: What is the saponification reaction? Write the balanced chemical equation for the saponification of ethyl ester (ethyl ethanoate) using sodium hydroxide. Explain how soap works to clean oily clothes. What is a micelle?
subject: science
topic: saponification and cleansing action of soap
answer:
Saponification: The process of hydrolysis of an ester with a base (alkali) to produce alcohol and the sodium salt of carboxylic acid (soap) is called saponification.

Chemical equation:
CH₃COOC₂H₅ + NaOH → CH₃COONa + C₂H₅OH
(Ethyl ethanoate) + (Sodium hydroxide) → (Sodium ethanoate/soap) + (Ethanol)

Industrially: Animal fats or vegetable oils (triglycerides) + NaOH → Soap + Glycerol.

Cleansing action of soap:
Soap molecules have two ends:
- Hydrophilic end (ionic -COO⁻Na⁺): attracted to water.
- Hydrophobic end (long carbon chain): attracted to oil/grease.

When soap is added to oily clothes in water:
1. The hydrophobic tails of soap molecules insert themselves into the oil/grease droplets.
2. The hydrophilic heads remain in water, pointing outward.
3. This forms a spherical cluster called a MICELLE — with oil trapped in the centre and ionic heads on the outside.
4. The micelles are suspended in water (emulsification) and can be rinsed away, removing the dirt/oil.

Micelle: A spherical cluster formed by soap molecules in which the hydrophobic (water-repelling) tails are oriented inward toward the trapped oil/grease, and hydrophilic (water-attracting) heads face outward toward water.

question: Write the chemical equations for the following reactions of ethanoic acid: (i) with sodium carbonate, (ii) with sodium hydroxide, (iii) with ethanol. Name the products in each case and identify which is the saponification and which is the esterification reaction.
subject: science
answer:
(i) With sodium carbonate (Na₂CO₃):
2CH₃COOH + Na₂CO₃ → 2CH₃COONa + H₂O + CO₂↑
Products: Sodium ethanoate (salt) + Water + Carbon dioxide gas.
The brisk effervescence of CO₂ is observed.

(ii) With sodium hydroxide (NaOH) — Neutralisation:
CH₃COOH + NaOH → CH₃COONa + H₂O
Products: Sodium ethanoate (salt) + Water.
This is a neutralisation reaction (acid-base reaction). Note: Saponification specifically refers to hydrolysis of an ester with alkali, not of an acid.

(iii) With ethanol (C₂H₅OH) — Esterification:
CH₃COOH + C₂H₅OH ⇌ CH₃COOC₂H₅ + H₂O
(Ethanoic acid) + (Ethanol) ⇌ (Ethyl ethanoate/ester) + (Water)
[Concentrated H₂SO₄ as catalyst, gentle heating]
Product: Ethyl ethanoate — a sweet-smelling ester.
This is called ESTERIFICATION (reaction of acid and alcohol to form ester and water).

Saponification is the reverse: hydrolysis of ethyl ethanoate with NaOH → sodium ethanoate + ethanol.
Esterification is (iii) above.

question: Draw the electron dot structure (Lewis structure) for: (i) methane (CH₄), (ii) ethene (C₂H₄), (iii) ethyne (C₂H₂). State the type of bond between carbon atoms in each molecule and define covalent bond.
subject: science
topic: carbon compound structures and covalent bonds
answer:
Covalent bond: A bond formed by mutual sharing of electron pairs between two atoms (of same or different non-metals) to attain the nearest noble gas configuration is called a covalent bond.

(i) Methane (CH₄):
Carbon (4 valence electrons) shares 1 electron with each of 4 hydrogen atoms.
Electron dot structure: C at centre with 4 single bonds to H (showing shared pairs).
Bond type: Single covalent bonds (C-H), tetrahedral shape.
No C-C bond.

(ii) Ethene (C₂H₄):
Two carbon atoms share 2 pairs of electrons between them.
Each C also bonds to 2 H atoms.
Electron dot structure: H₂C=CH₂ with 4 shared electrons shown between C-C.
Bond type: C=C double bond (one sigma + one pi bond).

(iii) Ethyne (C₂H₂):
Two carbon atoms share 3 pairs of electrons between them.
Each C bonds to 1 H atom.
Electron dot structure: H-C≡C-H with 6 shared electrons shown between C-C.
Bond type: C≡C triple bond (one sigma + two pi bonds).

Summary: CH₄ = single bond; C₂H₄ = double bond; C₂H₂ = triple bond.

question: What are homologous series? Give the homologous series of alkanes and alkanols (alcohols) starting from C1 to C5. State any four characteristics of a homologous series.
subject: science
answer:
Homologous Series: A series of organic compounds that have the same functional group and similar chemical properties, with each successive member differing by -CH₂- unit (14 mass units) is called a homologous series.

Alkanes (CₙH₂ₙ₊₂):
C1: CH₄ — Methane
C2: C₂H₆ — Ethane
C3: C₃H₈ — Propane
C4: C₄H₁₀ — Butane
C5: C₅H₁₂ — Pentane

Alkanols/Alcohols (-OH functional group, CₙH₂ₙ₊₁OH):
C1: CH₃OH — Methanol
C2: C₂H₅OH — Ethanol
C3: C₃H₇OH — Propanol
C4: C₄H₉OH — Butanol
C5: C₅H₁₁OH — Pentanol

Four characteristics of homologous series:
1. All members have the same functional group and general formula.
2. Successive members differ by -CH₂- (14 atomic mass units).
3. All members can be prepared by the same general method.
4. Physical properties (boiling point, melting point, solubility) show a gradual change with increase in molecular mass.
5. All members show similar chemical properties.

question: Explain the reflex arc with a neat labelled diagram. What is a reflex action? Give two examples. Why are reflex actions important?
subject: science
answer:
Reflex action: An automatic, rapid, involuntary response of the body to a stimulus — one that does not require conscious thinking — is called a reflex action.
Examples: (i) Withdrawing the hand on touching a hot object. (ii) Blinking of eyes in bright light. (iii) Knee-jerk reflex.

Reflex arc — the pathway followed by a nerve impulse during reflex action:
Diagram labels: Receptor (sense organ/skin), Sensory (afferent) neuron, Spinal cord (relay neuron in grey matter), Motor (efferent) neuron, Effector (muscle/gland).

Pathway:
Stimulus (e.g., touching hot object) → Receptor (skin) → Sensory neuron (carries impulse to spinal cord) → Relay neuron in spinal cord (integrates and relays) → Motor neuron (carries command away) → Effector muscle (contracts → hand withdraws).

Simultaneously, spinal cord sends signal to brain (for conscious awareness of pain), but the protective response occurs before the brain processes it.

Importance of reflex actions:
1. They protect the body from harm by producing extremely fast responses (faster than voluntary actions through the brain).
2. They reduce the burden on the brain by handling routine/emergency responses automatically.
3. They allow the brain to focus on complex decision-making while the spinal cord handles quick protective responses.

question: What are plant hormones? Name the different types of plant hormones and describe the role of each. How does auxin help in the bending of a plant shoot towards light?
subject: science
answer:
Plant hormones (phytohormones): Chemical substances produced naturally in plants that control and coordinate their growth, development, and responses to the environment.

Types and roles:
1. AUXIN (e.g., IAA): Promotes cell elongation. Responsible for phototropism and geotropism. Produced at shoot tips.
2. GIBBERELLIN: Promotes stem elongation, seed germination, and flowering. Helps plants grow taller.
3. CYTOKININ: Promotes cell division (cytokinesis). Delays ageing of leaves. Found in areas of rapid cell division (fruits, roots, seeds).
4. ABSCISIC ACID (ABA): Growth inhibitor. Causes dormancy of seeds and buds. Promotes stomatal closure during drought (stress hormone).
5. ETHYLENE: Promotes ripening of fruits, ageing, and abscission (leaf/fruit fall). Gaseous hormone.

Auxin and phototropism (bending towards light):
When light falls on one side of a shoot:
1. Auxin migrates from the illuminated side to the shaded side.
2. Higher auxin concentration on the shaded side causes more cell elongation on that side.
3. Lower auxin on the illuminated side means less elongation.
4. This unequal growth causes the shoot to bend towards the light source (positive phototropism).
This is detected by the shoot tip (apex), which is the site of auxin production.

question: What is the importance of variation to a species? Explain how sex is determined in human beings using a diagram. What is the role of X and Y chromosomes?
subject: science
answer:
Importance of variation:
1. Variations provide the raw material for evolution — beneficial variations are selected by natural selection over generations.
2. Variations allow a species to adapt to changing environments and avoid extinction.
3. Genetic diversity increases a population's resilience to diseases and environmental changes.
4. Variations can lead to formation of new species over time.

Sex determination in humans:
Humans have 23 pairs of chromosomes. The 23rd pair is the sex chromosomes:
- Females: XX (two X chromosomes)
- Males: XY (one X and one Y chromosome)

During gamete formation (meiosis):
- Females produce eggs, all carrying X chromosome.
- Males produce two types of sperm: 50% carry X and 50% carry Y.

Cross diagram:
Mother (XX) × Father (XY)
Eggs: X, X
Sperm: X, Y
Punnett square:
     X    X
  X [XX] [XX]  → Female
  Y [XY] [XY]  → Male

Result: 50% XX (female) : 50% XY (male)

Conclusion: Sex of a child is determined by the sperm (father), not the egg (mother). If X-bearing sperm fertilises the egg → girl (XX). If Y-bearing sperm fertilises → boy (XY). Hence, it is biologically incorrect to blame the mother for the sex of the child.

question: Draw ray diagrams to show the path of a ray of light when it passes from a denser medium to a rarer medium at angles (i) less than critical angle, (ii) equal to critical angle, (iii) greater than critical angle. Name the phenomenon in case (iii) and give one application.
subject: science
answer:
Setup: Light ray in glass (denser medium) striking the glass-air boundary.

Case (i) — Angle of incidence < Critical angle:
Ray refracts away from normal into air (bends away from normal as it goes to rarer medium). Partially reflected ray also exists but refracted ray is the main transmitted ray.
Diagram: Ray bends away from normal at boundary.

Case (ii) — Angle of incidence = Critical angle (C):
The refracted ray travels along the boundary (angle of refraction = 90°). A weak refracted ray grazes the surface.
Diagram: Refracted ray runs along the surface.

Case (iii) — Angle of incidence > Critical angle:
No refraction occurs. The entire light is reflected back into the denser medium (glass). This is TOTAL INTERNAL REFLECTION.
Diagram: Ray reflects back into glass following laws of reflection.

Total Internal Reflection: When light travels from a denser to a rarer medium at an angle of incidence greater than the critical angle, it is completely reflected back into the denser medium.

Application: Optical fibres — light signals are transmitted over long distances inside a glass/silica fibre by repeated total internal reflection, with negligible energy loss. Used in telecommunications, medical endoscopy.

question: An electric lamp of 100W, a toaster of 750W, and a water filter of 500W are connected to a 220V source. What is the resistance of each appliance? If all three are switched on simultaneously, what is the total current drawn from the source?
subject: science
answer:
Given: V = 220V
P_lamp = 100W, P_toaster = 750W, P_filter = 500W

Using P = V²/R → R = V²/P:

Resistance of lamp: R₁ = (220)² / 100 = 48400 / 100 = 484 Ω

Resistance of toaster: R₂ = (220)² / 750 = 48400 / 750 = 64.53 Ω

Resistance of water filter: R₃ = (220)² / 500 = 48400 / 500 = 96.8 Ω

All appliances are connected in parallel (as is standard in household circuits).

Total power consumed: P_total = 100 + 750 + 500 = 1350 W

Total current drawn: I = P_total / V = 1350 / 220 = 6.14 A

Alternatively, using I = P/V for each:
I_lamp = 100/220 = 0.45 A
I_toaster = 750/220 = 3.41 A
I_filter = 500/220 = 2.27 A
Total I = 0.45 + 3.41 + 2.27 = 6.13 A ≈ 6.14 A ✓

Note: Domestic circuits use parallel connections so each appliance gets full 220V and can be operated independently.

question: What is Joule's law of heating? Write its mathematical expression and explain each term. A wire of resistance 15Ω carries a current of 2A for 10 minutes. Calculate the heat produced in the wire.
subject: science
answer:
Joule's Law of Heating: The heat produced in a resistor is directly proportional to (i) the square of current (I²), (ii) the resistance (R) of the conductor, and (iii) the time (t) for which the current flows.

Mathematical expression: H = I²Rt
Where:
- H = heat produced (in joules, J)
- I = current through the conductor (in amperes, A)
- R = resistance of the conductor (in ohms, Ω)
- t = time for which current flows (in seconds, s)

Derivation basis: Work done in moving charge Q through potential difference V = QV = VIt = I²Rt (using V=IR). This work appears as heat energy.

Numerical:
Given: R = 15Ω, I = 2A, t = 10 min = 10 × 60 = 600 s.

H = I²Rt = (2)² × 15 × 600
H = 4 × 15 × 600
H = 36,000 J
H = 36 kJ

The heat produced in the wire is 36,000 joules (36 kJ).

question: Draw a labelled diagram of the excretory system in humans. Name two nitrogenous waste products excreted and name the organ that excretes each. What are the two main processes by which plants excrete waste?
subject: science
answer:
Diagram labels: Two kidneys (left and right), Renal artery (brings blood to kidney), Renal vein (takes purified blood away), Ureter (one from each kidney), Urinary bladder, Urethra.

Nitrogenous wastes and organs:
1. Urea — excreted by kidneys (filtered from blood in nephrons). Urea is produced in the liver from breakdown of excess amino acids.
2. Uric acid — excreted by kidneys (birds, reptiles excrete mainly uric acid; humans excrete small amounts).
3. CO₂ — excreted by lungs (though it is carbon-based, not nitrogenous).
4. Ammonia — excreted directly by aquatic organisms; in humans it is converted to urea in liver first.

Waste excretion in plants:
1. Gaseous exchange: CO₂ is excreted through stomata and lenticels during respiration. O₂ is excreted through stomata during photosynthesis.
2. Excretion into soil: Some plants excrete excess salts and organic waste through roots into the soil.
3. Storage in leaves: Some waste products (tannins, resins, gums, oils) are stored in leaves which fall off — this is an indirect excretory method.
4. Transpiration: Excess water is released as water vapour through stomata.

Plants have slow metabolism and produce less toxic waste, so they lack a dedicated excretory system unlike animals.

question: What is photosynthesis? Write the overall balanced chemical equation. Draw a labelled diagram of a chloroplast. Explain the role of light and CO₂ in photosynthesis.
subject: science
answer:
Photosynthesis: The process by which green plants (and other autotrophs) use sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide to produce glucose and oxygen is called photosynthesis.

Balanced equation:
6CO₂ + 6H₂O + Light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂
(Carbon dioxide + Water → Glucose + Oxygen)

Chloroplast diagram labels: Outer membrane, Inner membrane, Granum (stack of thylakoids), Thylakoid disc, Stroma (fluid-filled space), Lamellae (thylakoid membranes), Starch grains.

Role of light:
1. Light energy is absorbed by chlorophyll in the thylakoid membranes.
2. Light splits water molecules (photolysis): H₂O → 2H⁺ + ½O₂ + 2e⁻. The oxygen is released as a by-product.
3. Light energy is converted to chemical energy (ATP and NADPH) used in the next stage.

Role of CO₂:
1. CO₂ enters through stomata and diffuses into the mesophyll cells.
2. In the stroma (Calvin cycle/dark reaction), CO₂ is fixed (incorporated) using the ATP and NADPH produced by light reactions.
3. CO₂ is reduced to form glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆) — the food stored by the plant.

Conditions necessary for photosynthesis: Sunlight, chlorophyll, water, and carbon dioxide.

question: Explain anaerobic and aerobic respiration in humans with the help of chemical equations. Where in the cell does each process occur? What is the significance of ATP?
subject: science
answer:
Respiration: The process of breaking down glucose to release energy for cellular activities is called respiration.

Aerobic Respiration (in presence of oxygen):
Location: Cytoplasm (glycolysis) + Mitochondria (Krebs cycle + electron transport chain).
Equation:
C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + Energy (38 ATP)
(Glucose) + (Oxygen) → (Carbon dioxide) + (Water) + Energy

Steps: (1) Glycolysis: Glucose → 2 Pyruvate (in cytoplasm, anaerobic step). (2) Pyruvate enters mitochondria → acetyl CoA → Krebs cycle → CO₂ released. (3) Electron transport chain → water formed, large amount of ATP produced.

Anaerobic Respiration (in absence of oxygen):
Location: Cytoplasm only.
In yeast (fermentation):
C₆H₁₂O₆ → 2C₂H₅OH + 2CO₂ + Energy (2 ATP)
(Glucose → Ethanol + Carbon dioxide + Energy)

In human muscle cells (during vigorous exercise):
C₆H₁₂O₆ → 2C₃H₆O₃ + Energy (2 ATP)
(Glucose → Lactic acid + Energy)
Lactic acid accumulation causes muscle cramps.

Significance of ATP (Adenosine Triphosphate):
ATP is the universal energy currency of the cell. Energy released during respiration is stored in ATP's phosphate bonds. When energy is needed, ATP breaks down (ATP → ADP + Pᵢ + Energy), releasing energy for cellular work — muscle contraction, active transport, biosynthesis, nerve impulse, etc.

question: Draw a labelled diagram of a convex lens and show three principal rays used to draw ray diagrams. An object of 6 cm height is placed 20 cm in front of a convex lens of focal length 10 cm. Find the image distance, image height, and state the nature of the image.
subject: science
topic: convex lens ray diagram and numerical
answer:
Convex lens diagram labels: Principal axis, Optical centre (O), Focus F₁ (left), Focus F₂ (right), 2F₁ (left), 2F₂ (right), Object (AB).

Three principal rays:
1. A ray parallel to the principal axis → after refraction, passes through the principal focus F₂.
2. A ray passing through the optical centre O → passes straight without bending.
3. A ray passing through focus F₁ → after refraction, emerges parallel to the principal axis.

Numerical:
Given: Object height h = 6 cm, u = −20 cm (object on left), f = +10 cm (convex lens).

Using lens formula: 1/v − 1/u = 1/f
1/v − 1/(−20) = 1/10
1/v + 1/20 = 1/10
1/v = 1/10 − 1/20 = 2/20 − 1/20 = 1/20
v = +20 cm

Image is formed 20 cm on the other side of the lens (positive → real).

Magnification: m = v/u = 20/(−20) = −1
Image height = m × h = −1 × 6 = −6 cm

Nature of image: Real, inverted, same size as object, formed at 2F₂ (20 cm from lens on the other side).
This position (object at 2F) is used in photocopiers to produce same-size copies.

question: What is meant by dispersion of light? With a diagram, explain how a glass prism disperses white light into its constituent colours. Name the colours in order and state why violet deviates most.
subject: science
chapter: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
answer:
Dispersion of light: The splitting of white light into its constituent colours (spectrum) when it passes through a transparent medium like a prism is called dispersion of light.

Explanation with prism diagram:
1. White light enters one face of the triangular glass prism and strikes the second face.
2. At the first surface, light refracts (bends towards the normal as it enters denser glass).
3. Different colours of light have different wavelengths and travel at slightly different speeds in glass.
4. At the second surface, light refracts again (bends away from normal as it enters rarer air).
5. The bending is different for different wavelengths → they emerge at different angles.

Order of colours (VIBGYOR — from most to least deviation):
Violet → Indigo → Blue → Green → Yellow → Orange → Red

Violet deviates most, red deviates least.

Diagram labels: Incident white light, Prism (with angle A), Refracted white light splitting, Spectrum showing VIBGYOR from bottom (violet) to top (red).

Reason violet deviates most:
Violet light has the shortest wavelength and highest frequency. The refractive index of glass is highest for violet and lowest for red. Since deviation ∝ refractive index, violet is deviated (bent) the most and red the least. This is the basis for dispersion — different refractive indices for different colours.

question: What is electromagnetic induction? State Fleming's right-hand rule. Describe with a diagram how an AC generator works. What is the difference between AC and DC?
subject: science
answer:
Electromagnetic Induction: The phenomenon of producing an electric current in a conductor when it is placed in a changing magnetic field (or moved through a magnetic field) is called electromagnetic induction. Discovered by Michael Faraday.

Fleming's Right-Hand Rule (for generator): Stretch the thumb, index finger, and middle finger of the right hand mutually perpendicular. If the index finger points in the direction of the magnetic field (B) and the thumb points in the direction of motion of the conductor, then the middle finger points in the direction of the induced current.

AC Generator diagram labels: Armature coil (ABCD), Strong magnet (N and S poles), Slip rings (two rings), Carbon brushes, External circuit/load, Axis of rotation.

Working:
1. The rectangular armature coil is rotated in a magnetic field.
2. When coil sides AB and CD cut magnetic field lines, an EMF is induced (by Faraday's law).
3. As the coil rotates, the direction of induced current alternates every half rotation (180°).
4. Current flows: AB → external circuit → CD in first half; reverses in second half.
5. Slip rings maintain continuous contact with brushes, allowing current to flow to external circuit.
6. The output is alternating current (AC) — its direction reverses periodically.

AC vs DC:
- AC (Alternating Current): Direction reverses periodically; easier to transmit over long distances at high voltage; used in household supply (50 Hz in India).
- DC (Direct Current): Flows in one direction only; used in batteries, electronic devices.

question: Draw a labelled diagram showing the magnetic field lines around a current-carrying straight conductor. State the right-hand thumb rule. How does increasing the current change the magnetic field? What is solenoid and what is its magnetic field like?
subject: science
topic: magnetic field due to current and solenoid
answer:
Magnetic field around a straight conductor:
Diagram: Straight wire with current flowing upward (out of page). Concentric circular field lines around the wire. Arrow on circles showing direction (anticlockwise when current comes toward you).
Labels: Current direction (I), Magnetic field lines (B), Distance (r) from wire.

Right-Hand Thumb Rule: If a current-carrying conductor is held in the right hand such that the thumb points in the direction of current, then the fingers curl around the conductor in the direction of the magnetic field lines.

Effect of increasing current:
1. Increasing current → stronger magnetic field (B ∝ I).
2. The magnetic field intensity increases but the circular pattern of field lines remains the same.
3. Field lines become more closely spaced (higher flux density).

Solenoid: A long coil of wire consisting of many circular turns of insulated wire wound closely together is called a solenoid. It is essentially many loops of wire in series.

Magnetic field of a solenoid:
The magnetic field inside a solenoid resembles that of a bar magnet — it is uniform and parallel to the axis. One end acts as a North pole, the other as a South pole.
Diagram: Solenoid with current shown, parallel horizontal field lines inside, looped field lines outside emerging from N pole and entering S pole.

Strong electromagnets are made using solenoids with iron cores inside them.

question: What is an electric motor? Draw a labelled diagram of a DC electric motor and explain its working principle. State Fleming's left-hand rule.
subject: science
answer:
Electric motor: A device that converts electrical energy into mechanical (rotational) energy using the principle that a current-carrying conductor experiences a force when placed in a magnetic field.

Fleming's Left-Hand Rule: Stretch the thumb, index finger, and middle finger of the left hand mutually perpendicular. If the index finger points in the direction of the magnetic field (B) and the middle finger points in the direction of the current (I), the thumb points in the direction of the force (motion) on the conductor.

DC Motor diagram labels: Rectangular coil (ABCD), Strong permanent magnet (N and S poles), Split-ring commutator (two half-rings), Carbon brushes (X and Y), Battery and switch, Axis of rotation.

Working:
1. Current enters armature coil ABCD through brush X.
2. AB side (in magnetic field): Current in one direction → force upward (by Fleming's LHR).
3. CD side: Current in opposite direction → force downward.
4. These two opposing forces create a couple/torque that rotates the coil.
5. After half rotation, the split-ring commutator reverses the current direction through the coil, so the torque always acts in the same direction — keeping the coil rotating continuously.

Applications: Fans, pumps, washing machines, electric vehicles, mixers, drills.

question: What are biodegradable and non-biodegradable waste? Explain the importance of waste management. What is biomagnification? Give one example with a diagram showing how pesticides accumulate through a food chain.
subject: science
answer:
Biodegradable waste: Waste that can be broken down by microorganisms (bacteria, fungi) into simpler substances. Examples: kitchen waste, food scraps, paper, agricultural residue, animal dung.

Non-biodegradable waste: Waste that cannot be decomposed by microorganisms and persists in the environment for very long periods. Examples: plastics, glass, metals, DDT, synthetic fibres, nuclear waste.

Importance of waste management:
1. Prevents soil, water, and air pollution.
2. Conserves natural resources through recycling (e.g., paper recycling saves trees).
3. Prevents spread of disease by limiting breeding grounds for pathogens.
4. Reduces greenhouse gas emissions (methane from landfills).
5. Maintains ecological balance and protects biodiversity.

Biomagnification: The progressive increase in concentration of harmful, non-biodegradable substances (like DDT, heavy metals, pesticides) at successive trophic levels in a food chain is called biological magnification (biomagnification).

Diagram: Food chain with increasing DDT concentration:
Water/Soil → Phytoplankton (0.04 ppm) → Small fish (0.5 ppm) → Larger fish (2 ppm) → Fish-eating birds/humans (25 ppm).

Example: DDT sprayed on crops → washes into water → absorbed by algae → small fish eat algae → larger fish eat small fish → fish-eating birds (like ospreys) accumulate highest DDT levels → causes thinning of eggshells → population decline.

question: Draw a food web with at least three food chains linking grass, grasshoppers, frogs, snakes, and hawks. Explain the 10% energy rule. What happens to energy at each trophic level?
subject: science
answer:
Food web diagram:
Grass → Grasshopper → Frog → Snake → Hawk
Grass → Grasshopper → Frog → Hawk
Grass → Mouse → Snake → Hawk
(Arrows represent direction of energy flow — from prey to predator)

Trophic levels:
1. Grass — Producer (1st trophic level)
2. Grasshopper/Mouse — Primary consumer (2nd trophic level)
3. Frog — Secondary consumer (3rd trophic level)
4. Snake — Tertiary consumer (4th trophic level)
5. Hawk — Quaternary consumer (5th trophic level)

10% Energy Rule (Lindemann's Law):
Only 10% of the energy stored at one trophic level is transferred to the next trophic level. The remaining 90% is lost as:
- Heat (during respiration and metabolic processes)
- Excretory products
- Undigested materials

Example:
If grass (producers) store 1000J of energy:
→ Grasshoppers receive 100J (10% of 1000)
→ Frogs receive 10J (10% of 100)
→ Snakes receive 1J (10% of 10)
→ Hawks receive 0.1J (10% of 1)

Conclusion: This is why food chains rarely have more than 4-5 trophic levels — too little energy remains for higher consumers. It also explains why eating lower in the food chain (vegetarian diet) is more energy-efficient.

question: Explain the dihybrid cross performed by Mendel. A plant with round yellow seeds (RRYY) is crossed with a plant having wrinkled green seeds (rryy). Draw the Punnett square for the F2 generation and state the phenotypic ratio.
subject: science
topic: dihybrid cross and law of independent assortment
answer:
Dihybrid cross: Mendel's cross involving two pairs of contrasting characters (traits) simultaneously.

Parental cross: RRYY (Round, Yellow) × rryy (Wrinkled, Green)
R = round (dominant), r = wrinkled (recessive), Y = yellow (dominant), y = green (recessive)

F1 generation: All offspring = RrYy (Round, Yellow) — only dominant traits expressed.

F2 generation — Cross: RrYy × RrYy
Gametes produced by each parent: RY, Ry, rY, ry

Punnett square (4×4):
       RY      Ry      rY      ry
RY  [RRYY]  [RRYy]  [RrYY]  [RrYy]
Ry  [RRYy]  [RRyy]  [RrYy]  [Rryy]
rY  [RrYY]  [RrYy]  [rrYY]  [rrYy]
ry  [RrYy]  [Rryy]  [rrYy]  [rryy]

Phenotypic ratio in F2:
- Round Yellow (R_Y_): 9
- Round Green (R_yy): 3
- Wrinkled Yellow (rrY_): 3
- Wrinkled Green (rryy): 1
Ratio = 9:3:3:1

Law of Independent Assortment: Mendel concluded that different pairs of genes (R/r and Y/y) assort independently of each other during gamete formation — they are inherited independently. New combinations (Round Green and Wrinkled Yellow) appear in F2 that were not present in the parents.

question: Draw a labelled diagram showing the cross-section of a leaf. Explain how leaves are adapted for photosynthesis. What are stomata and what is their role in gaseous exchange?
subject: science
answer:
Cross-section of leaf diagram labels: Cuticle (waxy layer on epidermis), Upper epidermis (single layer), Palisade mesophyll (elongated cells, densely packed with chloroplasts), Spongy mesophyll (loosely packed, air spaces), Lower epidermis, Stomata (pores), Guard cells (kidney-shaped), Vascular bundle (xylem above, phloem below), Midrib.

Adaptations of leaves for photosynthesis:
1. Broad, flat lamina — maximises surface area for light absorption.
2. Thin structure — short distance for CO₂ to diffuse into all cells.
3. Transparent upper epidermis — allows light to reach mesophyll cells.
4. Palisade mesophyll cells — contain numerous chloroplasts, tightly packed to maximise light absorption.
5. Spongy mesophyll with air spaces — allows CO₂ and O₂ to move freely between cells.
6. Extensive network of veins — supply water and minerals (via xylem) and remove sugars (via phloem).
7. Stomata — allow entry of CO₂ and exit of O₂.

Stomata: Tiny pores on the surface of leaves (mostly on lower epidermis) surrounded by two guard cells.

Role in gaseous exchange:
1. CO₂ enters through stomata for photosynthesis.
2. O₂ produced during photosynthesis exits through stomata.
3. O₂ enters during respiration, CO₂ exits during respiration.
4. Water vapour exits during transpiration.

Opening/Closing: Guard cells swell (absorb water → turgid) → stomata open. Guard cells shrink (lose water → flaccid) → stomata close (during water stress, at night).

question: What are the differences between sexual and asexual reproduction? Describe binary fission in Amoeba and budding in Hydra with diagrams. What is the advantage of sexual reproduction over asexual reproduction?
subject: science
answer:
Differences:
Asexual reproduction: Only one parent, no gametes, offspring are genetically identical (clones), faster process. Examples: Amoeba, Hydra, Planaria.
Sexual reproduction: Two parents, involves gametes (male and female), offspring show genetic variation, slower process. Examples: humans, flowering plants.

Binary fission in Amoeba:
1. Amoeba's nucleus elongates and divides into two (karyokinesis).
2. Cytoplasm divides in the middle (cytokinesis).
3. Result: Two daughter cells, each genetically identical to the parent.
Diagram: Amoeba → nucleus starts elongating → two nuclei → cell pinches in middle → two daughter Amoeba cells.

Budding in Hydra:
1. A small outgrowth (bud) develops on the body wall of Hydra due to repeated cell division.
2. The bud grows, develops tentacles and a mouth.
3. The bud eventually detaches from the parent and becomes an independent Hydra.
Diagram: Hydra with bud growing from side → bud enlarging with tentacles → bud detaching → two separate Hydra.

Advantage of sexual reproduction over asexual:
1. Genetic variation — offspring are not identical to parents, creating diversity. This variation is the raw material for evolution and allows the species to adapt to changing environments.
2. Harmful mutations can be masked by dominant alleles from the other parent.
3. Disease resistance — greater genetic diversity means entire population is less likely to be wiped out by a single pathogen.
4. Evolutionary advantage — variation enables natural selection to operate.

question: What are fossil fuels? Why is there a need to conserve them? Explain any two sources of renewable energy, including their working principle. What are the limitations of solar energy and wind energy?
subject: science
topic: fossil fuels and renewable energy sources
answer:
Fossil fuels: Non-renewable energy sources formed from the decomposed remains of ancient organisms (plants and animals) buried under earth over millions of years. Examples: coal, petroleum, natural gas.

Need for conservation:
1. Non-renewable — took millions of years to form; cannot be replaced on human timescale.
2. Burning causes air pollution (SO₂, CO₂, NOₓ) contributing to acid rain and climate change.
3. CO₂ from combustion is the primary driver of global warming.
4. Supplies are finite — projected to deplete within decades to a few centuries.
5. Oil spills harm marine ecosystems.

Renewable Energy Sources:

1. Solar Energy (Solar Panels/Photovoltaic cells):
Principle: Photovoltaic cells convert sunlight directly into electricity using the photoelectric effect (light photons excite electrons in silicon wafers). Solar thermal collectors convert sunlight to heat water.
Application: Solar cookers, solar water heaters, solar power plants.
Limitations of solar energy: (i) Works only during daytime (not at night or on cloudy days). (ii) High initial cost of installation. (iii) Requires large land area for high output. (iv) Efficiency is still low (~20%).

2. Wind Energy:
Principle: Kinetic energy of wind turns large wind turbine blades, which rotate a generator to produce electricity. Wind farms are built in areas of consistent strong winds.
Application: Wind farms for electricity generation.
Limitations of wind energy: (i) Wind is intermittent and unpredictable. (ii) Wind farms require very large land areas. (iii) Noise pollution and visual impact. (iv) Threats to birds and wildlife. (v) Only viable in specific high-wind geographic locations.

question: State Ohm's law. Write the mathematical expression for it and define the SI unit of resistance.
subject: science
answer: Ohm's law: At constant temperature, the current (I) flowing through a conductor is directly proportional to the potential difference (V) across its ends. Mathematically: V = IR, where R is the resistance. SI unit of resistance is ohm (Ω). 1 Ω is the resistance of a conductor when a potential difference of 1 V causes a current of 1 A to flow through it.

question: Two resistors R₁ = 10 Ω and R₂ = 15 Ω are connected in series in a circuit with a 9 V battery. Calculate: (i) total resistance (ii) current in the circuit (iii) voltage across each resistor.
subject: science

question: Three resistors of 5 Ω, 10 Ω and 15 Ω are connected in parallel. Calculate the equivalent resistance of the combination.
subject: science

question: Prove that the equivalent resistance of three resistors R₁, R₂ and R₃ connected in series is R = R₁ + R₂ + R₃. Draw the circuit diagram.
subject: science
answer: In series, the same current I flows through each resistor. By Ohm's law: V₁ = IR₁, V₂ = IR₂, V₃ = IR₃. Total V = V₁ + V₂ + V₃ = I(R₁ + R₂ + R₃). If R is the equivalent resistance, V = IR. Therefore IR = I(R₁ + R₂ + R₃), so R = R₁ + R₂ + R₃.

question: Derive the expression for equivalent resistance when three resistors R₁, R₂ and R₃ are connected in parallel. Draw the circuit diagram.
subject: science
answer: In parallel, the same potential difference V appears across each resistor. By Ohm's law: I₁ = V/R₁, I₂ = V/R₂, I₃ = V/R₃. Total I = I₁ + I₂ + I₃ = V(1/R₁ + 1/R₂ + 1/R₃). If R is the equivalent resistance, I = V/R. Therefore 1/R = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂ + 1/R₃.

question: An electric iron of resistance 20 Ω takes a current of 5 A. Calculate: (i) power of the electric iron (ii) energy consumed in 30 minutes (in kWh and joules).
subject: science
answer:
(i) P = I²R = 5² × 20 = 500 W
(ii) Time = 30 min = 0.5 h
Energy = P × t = 500 × 0.5 = 250 Wh = 0.25 kWh
In joules: E = P × t = 500 × 1800 = 9,00,000 J = 9 × 10⁵ J

question: A bulb of resistance 500 Ω is connected to a 220 V supply. Calculate (i) current through the bulb (ii) power consumed (iii) energy used in 2 hours.
subject: science

question: How does the resistance of a wire depend on its (i) length (ii) cross-sectional area? Write the formula for resistivity. A copper wire of length 2 m has resistance 0.02 Ω. Find its resistivity if its cross-sectional area is 1 × 10⁻⁶ m².
subject: science
answer:
(i) R ∝ l (resistance is directly proportional to length)
(ii) R ∝ 1/A (resistance is inversely proportional to cross-sectional area)
Formula: R = ρl/A
Calculation: ρ = RA/l = (0.02 × 1 × 10⁻⁶)/2 = 1 × 10⁻⁸ Ω·m

question: A wire is stretched to double its original length. How does its resistance change? Explain.
subject: science
answer:
When wire is stretched to double length, volume remains constant. New length l' = 2l. Since volume = A × l = A' × 2l, new area A' = A/2.
New resistance R' = ρ(2l)/(A/2) = 4ρl/A = 4R.
The resistance becomes 4 times the original resistance.

question: Calculate the charge flowing through a circuit when a current of 10 A flows for 2 minutes. How many electrons flow through the circuit in this time? (Charge of electron = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C)
subject: science
answer:
Q = I × t = 10 × (2 × 60) = 10 × 120 = 1200 C
Number of electrons n = Q/e = 1200 / (1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹) = 7.5 × 10²¹ electrons

question: State Joule's law of heating. An electric heater draws 5 A from a 220 V supply. Calculate the heat produced in the heating element in 1 minute.
subject: science
answer:
Joule's law of heating: The heat produced in a resistor is directly proportional to (i) square of the current (H ∝ I²), (ii) resistance of the resistor (H ∝ R), (iii) time for which current flows (H ∝ t). Formula: H = I²Rt
Calculation: R = V/I = 220/5 = 44 Ω
H = I²Rt = 5² × 44 × 60 = 25 × 44 × 60 = 66,000 J = 6.6 × 10⁴ J

question: Why are alloys preferred over pure metals for making heating elements? Give two reasons.
subject: science
answer:
Alloys are preferred because:
(i) They have higher resistivity than pure metals, so they produce more heat for the same current.
(ii) They do not oxidize (burn) readily at high temperatures, so they have longer working life.
Example: Nichrome (alloy of Ni, Cr, Mn and Fe) is used in electric irons and heaters.

question: What is an electric fuse? Why is it always connected in series in an electric circuit? What material is used to make fuse wire and why?
subject: science
answer:
An electric fuse is a safety device that protects an electric circuit from excessive current (short circuit or overloading) by melting and breaking the circuit.
It is connected in series so that when it melts, the entire circuit is broken, protecting all appliances.
Fuse wire is made from an alloy of tin and lead (or pure tin/lead) because: (i) it has low melting point, so it melts quickly when current exceeds safe limit. (ii) it has high resistivity to generate enough heat.

question: Two identical resistors of 12 Ω each are connected: (i) in series, and (ii) in parallel, to a 6 V battery. Find the ratio of power consumed in the two cases.
subject: science

question: An electric lamp of 100 Ω, a toaster of resistance 50 Ω and a water filter of resistance 500 Ω are connected in parallel across 220 V. What is the total current drawn from the supply?
subject: science

question: How should four resistors of 8 Ω each be connected so that the effective resistance of the combination is 8 Ω? Draw the diagram.
subject: science
answer:
Connect four 8 Ω resistors in a 2-series, 2-parallel arrangement: make two groups of 2 resistors in series (each group = 16 Ω), then connect these two groups in parallel.
R_group = 8 + 8 = 16 Ω each
R_total = (16 × 16)/(16 + 16) = 256/32 = 8 Ω ✓

question: What is resistivity? Write its SI unit. How does resistivity of (i) metals (ii) semiconductors change with increase in temperature?
subject: science
answer:
Resistivity (ρ) is the resistance of a conductor of unit length and unit cross-sectional area. It is a property of the material. Formula: ρ = RA/l. SI unit: ohm-meter (Ω·m).
(i) Metals: Resistivity increases with increase in temperature (more lattice vibrations obstruct electron flow).
(ii) Semiconductors: Resistivity decreases with increase in temperature (more charge carriers become available).

question: A wire of resistance 6 Ω is connected to a 12 V battery. Calculate (i) current (ii) power dissipated (iii) heat produced in 10 minutes.
subject: science

question: Draw a circuit diagram showing two resistors R₁ and R₂ connected in parallel with a battery, ammeter and voltmeter in appropriate positions. State how ammeter and voltmeter are connected.
subject: science
answer: Ammeter is connected in series in the main circuit (to measure total current). Voltmeter is connected in parallel across the resistors (to measure potential difference). Ammeter has very low resistance; voltmeter has very high resistance so they do not significantly change the circuit.

question: The V-I graph for two resistors A and B are straight lines. Resistor A has a steeper slope than B. Which has greater resistance? Justify.
subject: science
answer: Resistor A (steeper slope) has greater resistance. In a V-I graph, the slope = V/I = R. A steeper slope means a higher ratio of V to I, which means higher resistance. Alternatively: the inverse of the slope of I-V graph gives resistance; in a V-I graph, the slope itself is R.

question: What are the advantages of connecting electrical appliances in parallel in domestic circuits rather than in series?
subject: science
answer:
Advantages of parallel connection:
(i) Each appliance gets the same voltage (220 V) as the supply, so works at its rated capacity.
(ii) If one appliance fails, others continue to work independently.
(iii) Different appliances with different power ratings can be connected as each draws current according to its own resistance.
(iv) Total resistance decreases, allowing more appliances to be added.

question: A copper wire has diameter 0.5 mm and resistivity 1.6 × 10⁻⁸ Ω·m. What length of wire is needed to make a resistance of 10 Ω?
subject: science

question: Two resistors of 2 Ω and 4 Ω are connected (i) in series, (ii) in parallel across a 6 V battery. Calculate the heat produced in the 4 Ω resistor in 5 seconds in each case.
subject: science
answer:
(i) Series: I = V/(R₁+R₂) = 6/6 = 1 A; H = I²Rt = 1² × 4 × 5 = 20 J
(ii) Parallel: V across 4 Ω = 6 V; H = V²t/R = 36 × 5 / 4 = 45 J

question: What is the function of an earth wire in domestic circuits? Why is it necessary to earth metallic bodies of electrical appliances?
subject: science
answer: The earth wire (green insulation) connects the metallic body of an appliance to the earth (zero potential). If there is a fault and current leaks to the metallic body, the earth wire provides a low-resistance path for the current to flow safely into the ground, preventing electric shock to the user. This is a safety measure to protect users from accidental electrocution.

question: State the difference between overloading and short circuit. How does a fuse protect against each?
subject: science
answer:
Overloading: occurs when too many appliances are connected to the same circuit, drawing more current than the wire can safely carry. Short circuit: occurs when the live and neutral wires touch each other directly (no resistance), causing a very large current to flow suddenly.
In both cases, the excessive current heats the fuse wire beyond its melting point, causing it to melt and break the circuit, thus preventing fire and damage to appliances.

question: An electric bulb is rated 60 W at 220 V. How many units of energy does it consume in 30 days if used 8 hours daily? Also find its resistance.
subject: science

question: A student connects a 6 Ω resistor in series with a parallel combination of two resistors X and Y. If X = 4 Ω and Y = 12 Ω, and the battery is 12 V, find: (i) equivalent resistance of the full circuit (ii) total current (iii) current through each parallel branch.
subject: science
answer:
(i) Parallel combination of X and Y: 1/R_p = 1/4 + 1/12 = 3/12 + 1/12 = 4/12; R_p = 3 Ω
Total R = 6 + 3 = 9 Ω
(ii) I = V/R = 12/9 = 4/3 ≈ 1.33 A
(iii) Voltage across parallel combination = I × R_p = (4/3) × 3 = 4 V
I_X = 4/4 = 1 A; I_Y = 4/12 = 0.33 A
